clinical_rationale.summary: The MS Brain MRI protocol prioritizes lesion detection and characterization across white matter, cortical, and normal-appearing brain parenchyma. The departmental 2025 reference document provides high parameter density (12 technical terms) with explicit TE/TR values for all five sequences, making it the primary benchmark for site-specific parameters. PubMed evidence from gold-standard journals (Radiology, AJNR, RadioGraphics, European Radiology, Investigative Radiology) supports advanced imaging approaches including 3D FLAIR for lesion detection, T1-based microstructural mapping, SWI for venocentric lesion characterization, and DWI for acute demyelination, but the abstracts are largely parameter-sparse (density 0–1), so the reference document governs specific TE/TR values. Emerging research highlights the promise of UTE myelin imaging and 7T acquisitions, but these remain investigational and are not incorporated into the routine clinical protocol.
clinical_rationale.key_changes: Recent literature (2020–2025) increasingly emphasizes 3D isotropic FLAIR as the cornerstone sequence for MS lesion detection, replacing older 2D FLAIR due to superior sensitivity for juxtacortical and infratentorial lesions as endorsed by MAGNIMS/CMSC consensus. Quantitative T1 mapping and sodium MRI (7T) are emerging as sensitive microstructural biomarkers for disability tracking in RRMS, though not yet standard of care. UTE sequences for direct myelin imaging (Radiology 2020, PMID 32779970) represent a promising research frontier. SWI has become increasingly incorporated into clinical MS protocols for identifying the central vein sign and paramagnetic rim lesions, which are now recognized diagnostic and prognostic markers. The 2025 departmental protocol reflects these trends by including 3D FLAIR, 3D MPRAGE, SWI, and DWI as core sequences.
